Gunnar Myrdal (i) Backwash Effects are the causes of uneven economic development within and between nations. (ii) Suggestions to strengthen the Spread Effects for reducing regional inequalities.

Gunnar Myrdal is best known in development economics for his challenge to equilibrium theory and the notion of “circular and cumulative causation”. Myrdal was opposed to the neoclassical equilibrium theory and argued that the forces of demand and supply will interact with each other to produce cumulative movements away from the spatial equilibrium. His ideas of economic underdevelopment and development are centered around regional inequalities on the national and international levels. Myrdal’s model is one of many that can be used to understand the perpetuation of development gap and the divergence between the centre and the periphery countries. Myrdal considers that the existence of dualism not only retards the development of backward regions but also slows up the development of the whole economy. In this context, explain the Myrdal concept of circular and cumulative causation in terms of Backwash effect and spread effect. Is your government creating enough infrastructures for reducing the regional inequalities?

Gunnar Myrdal is best known in development economics for his challenge to equilibrium theory and the notion of “circular and cumulative causation”. Myrdal was opposed to the neoclassical equilibrium theory and argued that the forces of demand and supply will interact with each other to produce cumulative. movements away from the spatial equilibrium. His ideas of economic underdevelopment and development are centered around regional inequalities on the national and international levels. Myrdal’s model is one of many that can be used to understand the perpetuation of development gap and the divergence between the centre and the periphery countries. Myrdal considers that the existence of dualism not only retards the development of backward regions but also slows up the development of the whole economy. In this context, explain the Myrdal concept of circular and cumulative causation in terms of Backwash effect and spread effect. Is your government creating enough infrastructures for reducing the regional inequalities?

Gunnar Myrdal described the regional inequalities at the national and international levels in his theory of circular cumulative causation. He built his theory of economic development and underdevelopment around the idea of regional inequalities at national and international levels. He was opposed to the neo- classical theory of equilibrium. The key elements of Myrdal’s thesis are cumulative causation, Backwash Effects, Spread Effects and government interventions. Myrdal argued that trade would benefit the prosperous regions unless there are government interventions, infrastructural development and targeted investments. Given this copseatualaiindersianding dertake an (i) Backwash Effects with the forces of demang and supply. (ii) Need of the egalitarian policies by the governmens of underdeveloped countries to weaken the Backwash Effects and strengthen the Spread Effect.

The Swedish economist Gunnar Myrdal built his theory of economic development and underdevelopment around the idea of regional inequalities at national and international levels. He was opposed to the neo- classical theory of equilibrium. The key elements of Myrdal’s thesis are cumulative causation, Backwash Effects, Spread Effects and government interventions. Myrdal argued that trade would benefit the prosperous regions unless the governments of the backward regions take suitable policy measures. Given this conceptual backdrop, explain the following points. (i) How backwash effects remain responsible for uneven economic development within and between nations. i (ii) Give your suggestions to your government to strengthen the spread effects for reducing regional inequalities.

() Role of WTO in promoting trade- led development (i) Dispute settlement system of WTO

World Trade Organisation (WTO) is the successor to the GATT and came into existence on January 1, 1995. The Uruguay Round of Trade negotiations gave the final shape to the formation of WTO. The primary mission of WTO is to promote international trade by reducing trade barriers, enforcing trade rules and providing a platform for trade negotiations and dispute settlement among its member countries. Having the idea of manifold roles of WTO in promoting international trade, you elaborate the following roles of WTO. (i) Trade Development (ii) Dispute Settlement — @ SECTION-D

World Trade Organisation (WTO) came into existence [ on January 1, 1995. The Uruguay Round of Trade negotiations (1986- led to the creation of WTO. It superseded the GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade) that served nearly fifty years for global trade. WTO plays a crucial role in regulating and promoting global trade including services, Intellectual Property Rights and Trade Related Investments. It has around 164 member countries at present. It provides special attention to the developing countries to participate effectively in the global trade. The roles of WTO contribute to the promotion of international trade. On this background, elaborate the following important functions of WTO. (i) Trade Development (ii) Dispute Settlement Write two suggestions that would help the WTO in enhancing global trade. inalion ssh a

Prebisch-Singer Thesis (i) Reasons behind the secular deterioration of the TOT for the primary product exporting countries Two suggestions for improving the TOT. (ii) Arguments of Prebisch-Singer on the TOT for oil-exporting developing countries

The Prebisch-Singer thesis explains the terms of trade between primary commodities and manufactured goods. The non-oil exporting developing countries have to export large amount of their primary products in order to import manufactured goods from developed countries. The deterioration of terms of trade has been a. major obstructive factor in the development of these developing countries. With this consideration, provide a detailed explanation to Prebisch-Singer thesis on the terms of trade. Will the ISRAEL and HAMAS war in the Middle-East create further deterioration in the terms of trade?

Prebisch-Singer thesis discusses about the long-term trade dynamics between the developed and developing countries. The fundamental argument of the thesis is that the developing countries export primary products and import the manufactured products from the developed countries. They explained as to how the developing countries receive less values for their exports relative to the cost of imports leading to slow economic growth in these countries. It is apprehended that a full-scale war escalation between ISRAEL and IRAN would significantly impact the global terms of trade as IRAN is a major player in the global oil market. Considering the importance of international trade and the war situation in the Middle East you do a submission to the following discussions of Prebisch and Singer in their thesis. (i) Underlying assumption of the thesis (ii) Consider a developing country that basically exports primary goods. Explain how a decline in global prices primary goods would affect its terms of trade.

W.W.Rostow asserts that countries go through five linear stages of growth. Rostow’s doctrine describes the transition from underdevelopment to development by these stages which all countries have to pass through. Rostow penned that all countries exist somewhere on this linear spectrum and climb upward through each stage in the development process. Give a lucid report on Rostow’s model of development with special reference to your economy.

WW.Rostow has put emphasis on social and institutional factors as important elements of economic development. His growth theory has largely influenced the field of development economics. Rostow’s doctrine describes the transition from underdevelopment to development in terms of a series of linear stages of varying length. His ideas rest on the fact that agriculture has to supply more food to the growing towns and out of large incomes that are yielded by increased agricultural productivity must come an expanding market for manufactures and a large flow of savings. He argued that economic growth was a multi- staged process stimulated by a wide spread desire for improvement of life as well as the search for profits. On this background, explain the stages of economic growth as propounded by Rostow. Write your impression about this growth model to be used as a guide for underdeveloped countries.

Externalities refer to the uncompensated impact of one agent's action on the third parties. These impacts can be positive or negative and may influence individuals,. business or society as a whole. Externalities are a type of market failure because the price mechanism in a. market does not fully reflect all the costs and benefits associated with a particular economic transaction. keeping in view the various externalities, discuss the government mechanisms to control and adjust externalities.

Externalities pose fundamental economic policy problems when individuals, households and firms do not internalize the indirect costs and benefits from their economic transactions. The resulting wedges between social and private costs or returns lead to inefficient market outcomes. In some cases they may prevent markets from emerging. Although there is market based corrective solutions, government interventions is often required to ensure that benefits and costs are fully internalized. Consumption, production and investment decisions of individuals, households and firms often affect people not directly involved in the transactions. Those effects are sometimes tiny. But when they are large they can become problematic. The main reasons of government interventions in the economic sphere are the externalities. Most externalities fall into the category of so-called technical externalities but the price of the product does not take those externalities into account. As a result, there are differences between private returns or costs and the returns or costs to society as a [ c+ - a + J positive - a + externalities and negative externalities with four suitable empirical examples from each. Suggest two potential measures to your government that could address negative externalities.

Externalities pose fundamental economic policy problems when individuals, households and firms do not internalize the indirect costs and benefits from their economic transactions. The resulting wedges between social and private costs or returns lead to inefficient market outcomes. In some cases they may prevent markets from emerging. Although there is market based corrective solutions, government interventions is often required to ensure that benefits and costs are fully internalized. Consumption, production and investment decisions of individuals, households and firms often affect people not directly involved in the transactions. Those effects are sometimes tiny. But when they are large they can become problematic. The main reasons of government interventions in the economic sphere are the externalities. Most externalities fall into the category of so-called technical externalities but the price of the product does not take those externalities into account. As a result, there are differences between private returns or costs and the returns or costs to society as a whole. On these evidences, explain positive externalities and negative externalities with four suitable empirical examples from each. Suggest two potential measures to your government that could address negative externalities.

Positive externalities benefit the society beyond the ] individuals or firms involved in the actions whereas, negative externalities impose costs on the society that are neither compensated by the producers or the consumers. For achieving a more sustainable society, negative externalities should be reduced or eliminated through regulations, taxes, subsidies and technological innovations. Negative externalities create market failures by inefficient resource allocation and can lead to overproduction or overconsumption of such externalities. Negative externalities bring about environmental damages that the society can’t afford in the long-run. Government can implement policy measures to internalize the costs of negative externalities. Government can also encourage sustainable practices. As an engineering student you are concerned about your commitment towards balancing the technical expertise and ethical practices for reducing the environmental damages. On the basis of this discussion you clarify the following government mechanisms for adjusting the externalities. Ricardo’s ideas (i) Behaviour of rent, wage and profit with a suitable figure using AP and MP curves (ii) Stationary state with graph Relation between MP and subsistence Wage before the stationary state

David Ricardo was a British cconomist who contributed in the field of comparative advantage international trade and model of income distribution. His ideas on economic development are available in his famous book “Principles of Political Economy and Taxation”. His theory of comparative advantage laid the foundation for the modern prospect of international trade. He emphasized on the role of the capitalist in economic development. With this impression, illustrate the following concepts propounded by Ricardo. (i) Distribution of national output among three different agents of production with the help of Marginal Product (MP) and Average Product (AP) curves. (ii) Show the point of stationary state with the help of MP and AP curves where the profit is squeezed to zero.

Adam Smith was a strong advocate of free market and free trade. He recognized the role increasing returns in the growth and development process based on the principle of division of labour. The notion of increasing return based on the division of labour lay at the heart of his optimistic vision of economic progress as a self-generating process. Having understood this, give a proper description to Smith’s ideas on Division of labour, Increasing Return, Free Trade and Trade Cycle.

Adam Smith was among the first classical economist of his time to declare that wealth is created through productive labour and the self interest motivates people. to put their resources to the best use. He viewed the government regulations as potentially detrimental to economic growth. In the Wealth of Nations Smith argued that wealth existed in the value a commercial society created through production and trade. Smith’s ideas formed the basis for how most countries determine their Wealth to this day. He established the concepts of specialization and division of labour and pleaded for a macro approach to economic development. Smith’s Laissez-Faire approach to economic policy in the 18" century came at the time when government discouraged the international trade. On this backdrop, do a precise submission of the following ideas of Adam Smith e Increasing Return * Free Trade * Trade Cycle Are these determinants of Adam Smith on economic development influencing the policy makers of developing countries for accelerating their pace of development?

Ideas of Malthus on (i) Raising Effective Demand (i) Balanced Growth

Thomas Malthus is well known for his work on population growth and its implications for economic development. He predicted that population growth will outstrip food supply. Some developing countries are still showing Malthusian characteristics and some economists predict of Malthusian world in future. It is rightly said that the ghost of Malthus still hunts many developing country today because of his views on population growth. Being the follower of Thomas Malthus, you CHE suggested Page 4 / T — @ + by him to promote economic growth and development of a country.

What do you mean by economic development of a country?

Development refers to the improvement of well-being and quality of life of a nation over time. The term development is used to describe the economic and social transformation within a country. Economic development is a multi-dimensional concept that goes beyond mere economic growth and is indicative of social, political and environmental aspects. As a development economist you explain the economic forces that determine the economic development of your country. 1-2 +] pa - Q +
Answer the following questions. \ (a) What do you mean by economic development of a country?
Answer the following questions. \ (a) What do you mean by economic development of a country?
Give two important arguments to justify that international trade is an engine of economic growth.

International trade is associated to exports or imports at least between two different countries. Taking all the developing countries together it is not true that the world as a whole is methodically polarized into two camps i.e developing countries producing and exporting only primary products and developed countries producing and exporting only manufactured goods. In effect, a good amount of trade in both manufactures and primary products goes on among developing and developed countries alike. Historically, trade has acted as a powerful engine of economic growth both for developing and developed countries. In the nineteenth century the demand for food and raw materials in Europe and in Britain brought prosperity to U:2024/SOT Spring End Semester Exam YM ’ countries such as Canada, Argentina, South Arica, Australia and New Zealand. Alfred Marshall, an English neoclassical economist rightly wrote “the causes which determine the economic progress of nations belong to the study of international trade.” With this notion, bring your arguments to advocate that your country should engage in international trade for. achieving economic growth.

In effect, a good amount of trade in both manufactures and primary products goes on among developing and developed countries alike. Historically, trade has acted as a powerful engine of economic growth both for developing and developed countries. In the nineteenth century the demand for food and raw materials in Europe and in Britain brought prosperity to countries such as Canada, Argentina, South Arica, Australia and New Zealand. Alfred Marshall, an English neoclassical economist rightly wrote “the causes which determine the economic progress of nations belong to the study of international trade.” With this notion, bring your arguments to advocate that your country should engage in international trade for. achieving economic growth.

What is Sustainable Development? Write two characteristics of public goods.

Sustainable development aims at creating sustainable improvements in the quality of life of all people as the principal goal of development policy. Besides increasing economic growth and basic needs, the aim of lifting standard of living including better health, education opportunities, good public life and clean environment are among other objectives of sustainable development. The United Nations has 17 sustainable Development Goals (SDGS). In this connection, you write the goals and objectives of first eight SDGs that leave no one behind in the development process. K412023 0 Aun Ev Semcst

Sustainable development has perhaps become the most important approach to considering the environment and development. The World Development Report is advocating for widening the horizon of sustainable development from sustainable economic development to sustainable human development to have its long term implications. Sustainable development has become a standard model for thinking about the environment, development and the economy. The United Nations 2005 World Summit reaffirmed its commitment to ’ achieve the goal of sustainable development in its three components i.e. economic development, social oF development and environmental protection. The new Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) which outmoded the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) are an important set of policy objectives across a wide range of economic, social and environmental contexts. The present pattern of economic growth may seriously degrade the environment and may be unsustainable, as the environment can’t support economic growth forever. Many economists are concerned that past and present economic policies have usually been connected with providing equilibrium economic growth and have not attempted to the existence of ecological conditions required to support the human life at a specified level of well-being through future generations. You being a development economist write the last eight SDGs with their specific targets that leave no one behind in the development process. Bring out two achievements of our countrv in this direction.

The new Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) which outmoded the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) are an important set of policy objectives across a wide range of economic, social and environmental contexts. The present pattern of economic growth may seriously degrade the environment and may be unsustainable, as the environment can’t support economic growth forever. Many economists are concerned that past and present economic policies have usually been connected with providing equilibrium economic growth and have not attempted to the existence of ecological conditions required to support the human life at a specified level of well-being through future generations. You being a development economist write the last eight SDGs with their specific targets that leave no one behind in the development process. Bring out two achievements of our country in this direction.

Less developed countries often face a range of challenges that hinder their progress in various aspects including economic growth, education, healthcare and infrastructure. Efforts are needed through targeted development interventions to improve education, healthcare, per capita income and economic opportunities and achieving the well-being of their population. In this context, discuss Low Level of Living and Extreme Income Inequalities observed in less developed countries.

Poverty and hunger are two significant global issues that affected the developed and developing countries alike. Hunger and malnutrition resulting from poverty can lead to multiple health problems. They also restrict the access to education. Poverty and hunger are influenced by complex socioeconomic factors. Addressing these interconnected issues need coordinated efforts at local, national and global levels to bring more resilient communities. Holistic and sustainable solutions addressing economic, social, environmental and political dimensions are the need of the hour. Illustrate the dimensions and indicators of Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) and Global Hunger Index (GHI) as you realize the importance of addressing these issues. " re.

Population and economic development are interlinked. Population and environment linkage indicate that population dynamics and environmental conditions influence each other. The growth and distribution of human population have a significant impact on natural environment. Understanding the interplay between population dynamics and environment is crucial for formulating policies that promote sustainable development. Slowing down of population growth rate would help in controlling the intensification of many environmental issues. Under this given condition, write a note on population-environment interactions that impact the economic development of a country.

We should be aware of the environmental issues as our environment is constantly changing. Various environmental protection programmes are practiced at the individual, organizational and governmental levels with the aim of establishing a balance between man and environment. Much of the concern over environmental issues arises from the fear that we may reach a limit to the number of people whose needs can be met by earth’s finite resources. Some economists argue that this fear is unfounded as technological innovations can fulfill the needs of the growing population. Currently Bangalore is facing worsening water crisis putting India’s IT capital high and dry. Explosive growth of the IT capital has resulted in the concretization of natural landscape that reduces the ground water recharge leading to less water percolation. The notable environmental activist Sonam Wangchuk’s concerns on climate fast is an opportunity to look at Ladakh’s delicate future. It is argued that economic growth and development has caused serious environmental damages and that the current state of environment will constrain the future economic development. Based on this discussion, you address the population-Environment linkages and their implications for economic development.

Appropriate answer to each question carries 1 mark
Show the share of landlord with a suitable diagram as explained by David Ricardo in his theory of development.

David Ricardo’s ideas on economic development are available in his famous book “Principles of Political Economy and Taxation” in which he predicted that capitalist economies would end up in a stationary state with no growth, owing to diminishing return in agriculture. In Ricardian model like Smith’s growth and development are a function of capital accumulation. - According to him profits are squeezed between subsistence wages and the rent paid to the landlord. Based on this discussion, clarify the following ideas of David Ricardo on development (i) Capital Accumulation (ii) Behaviour of rent, wage and profit with correct diagram (iii) International Trade
